rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is a common, uncomplicated atopic dermatitis flare with normal vitals, no fever and no features of infection — benign and telehealth-manageable. It is not an emergency and does NOT warrant ED/000 (the source note's 'urgent after-hours' framing reflects in-person billing justification, not true acuity). T2 (prompt GP / same telehealth encounter, 24–48 h) is appropriate because the severe flare needs prescription-strength topical therapy and a documented plan, and because the excoriated skin should be reviewed to confirm response and exclude evolving infection. Self-care alone (T0/T1) under-serves the severity and the need for a prescription and safety-netting, while any tier of T3+ over-triages a benign condition.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Widespread painful blisters or clustered punched-out sores, especially with fever or feeling generally unwell (eczema herpeticum)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to an emergency department without delay
escalation_edges.rationale: Eczema herpeticum is a dermatological emergency (disseminated HSV) requiring urgent assessment and systemic aciclovir; topical steroid must be stopped.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop widespread painful blisters or punched-out sores, or a fever with the rash, go to an emergency department straight away — do not keep using the steroid cream.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Skin starts weeping thick yellow fluid, develops honey-coloured crusts or pustules, becomes hot/painful/spreading, or you develop a fever (secondary bacterial superinfection)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same-day urgent GP / urgent care review
escalation_edges.rationale: Secondary staphylococcal superinfection of excoriated eczema needs prompt antibiotic treatment and review.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the skin starts weeping yellow fluid, crusting over, or becomes hot, spreading and painful, or you get a fever, see a doctor the same day — you may need antibiotics.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The flare is not improving after 5–7 days of treatment, or the steroid stops being effective
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Book GP review within a few days
escalation_edges.rationale: Non-response prompts reassessment — review adherence/technique, reconsider scabies or contact dermatitis, and plan step-up or specialist referral.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If it's not clearly better in 5–7 days, or the cream stops working, book a GP review so we can reassess and adjust the plan.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with high distress and severe sleep deprivation
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give clear written safety-net instructions and ensure the patient knows exactly which changes mean 'seek help today' versus 'routine review', since there is no one else at home to notice deterioration.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Start the topical steroid on the active patches and use the greasy fragrance-free moisturiser all over frequently; the itch and redness should ease within 3–5 days. Take a sedating antihistamine at night to help you sleep. Keep the daily moisturiser going long-term even once it settles. Arrange a GP review in about 7 days.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: GP or telehealth review within 24–48 h to confirm the plan and in about 7 days to reassess
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: For the emergency red flags (widespread painful blisters, weeping/crusting with fever, hot spreading painful skin) attend an emergency department or urgent care; otherwise these do not need after-hours care.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the flare description, the topical corticosteroid course and emollient plan, and the safety-net triggers so the regular GP can plan long-term maintenance and consider step-up therapy if needed.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Early eczema herpeticum or subtle secondary infection cannot be fully excluded on video.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the flare, screen explicitly for the mimic features, and give unambiguous escalation triggers with timeframes; arrange timely review.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either dismissing the flare as trivial with no safety-net, or over-reacting by sending a well, afebrile patient with classic eczema to the emergency department.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as trivial self-care with no prescription and no safety-netting for eczema herpeticum or bacterial superinfection.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving eczema herpeticum or superinfection is missed because the patient was given no escalation triggers and no follow-up.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating a well, afebrile patient with a classic, uncomplicated eczema flare to the emergency department or calling an ambulance — unnecessary ED attendance, patient alarm, and wasted emergency resources for a telehealth-manageable condition.
